title: Making sugar syrup for bees
subtitle: Spring feed, autumn feed, and how the ratio changes with the purpose.
excerpt: The same recipe used in spring to stimulate the colony is not the right feed for autumn stores. A thin syrup encourages brood-rearing; a thick one provides winter reserves. This tutorial covers both ratios, the equipment needed, and what can go wrong when syrup is made or fed incorrectly.
sourceNotes:
National Bee Unit, Beekeeper's Toolkit (2024 edition), feeding section. https://www.nationalbeeunit.com
British Beekeepers Association (BBKA) advice sheets on colony feeding.
Frank Pearson, Bee-keeping for All (1923), chapter on feeding bees, Internet Archive.

glossaryTerms.definition: The proportion of sugar to water by weight. A 1:1 ratio (1 kg sugar to 1 litre water) is thin feed used in spring. A 2:1 ratio (2 kg sugar to 1 litre water) is thick feed used for autumn stores.
The full social unit of a hive: one mated queen, several thousand worker bees, and seasonally a few hundred drones, all sharing a single nest.
The shallower box placed above the queen excluder where bees store surplus honey for harvest. Remove supers before feeding syrup to avoid contaminating the honey crop.

content.text: Bees cannot overwinter on an empty hive, and a weak spring

needs help before the first nectar flow. Sugar syrup is the standard substitute for nectar: cheap, safe, and readily taken by the bees. The concentration you use matters because bees process thin and thick syrup differently.

attrs.title: Remove supers before feeding
attrs.body: Never feed syrup with honey supers in place on the hive. Bees will move syrup into the supers, contaminating the honey crop. Remove all supers before autumn feeding, and do not replace them until the feeding programme is complete. Spring feeding of a thin syrup to stimulate brood-rearing is less of a risk if the supers have not yet been added for the season.

content.text: sets what the bees do with it. Thin 1:1 syrup (equal weights of sugar and water) is easy for the bees to process and stimulates the queen to increase her laying rate. Use it in early spring when you want brood-rearing to accelerate, or as an emergency feed for a small colony at any time of year. Thick 2:1 syrup (two parts sugar to one part water by weight) is harder to process and closer in moisture content to ripe honey. The bees store it as a winter food reserve. Use it from late August through September, before the cluster forms.

White granulated sugar is the correct ingredient. Brown sugar, raw cane sugar, and icing sugar all contain impurities that bees cannot digest, leading to dysentery in winter. Never use honey from an unknown source as a feed supplement, as it may carry disease.

For a 1:1 batch: measure 1 kg of white sugar and 1 litre of warm water. Stir until the sugar is fully dissolved. For a 2:1 batch: measure 2 kg of sugar and 1 litre of water. The water needs to be hot, around 60 to 70 degrees, to dissolve the higher concentration. Do not boil the syrup, as boiling converts sucrose into compounds the bees cannot process well.
Allow the syrup to cool to room temperature before putting it in the feeder. Hot syrup placed in a feeder damages the colony.

A rapid feeder is the most practical option for autumn bulk-feeding. Fill it once or twice a week through September. The bees work the syrup down into the frames quickly and cap it off. A contact feeder (an inverted bucket with a perforated lid) suits smaller quantities; the bees sip through the perforations by surface tension. Neither type should be left empty for more than a day, as bees guard an empty feeder as vigorously as a full one and it triggers robbing from other colonies.

before fitting the feeder. Place the rapid feeder directly above the crown-board hole over the cluster. A spare eke (a thin wooden spacer box) around it stops the colony getting to the top of the feeder from the outside.

items.symptom: Syrup not taken after two or three days
items.cause: A strong nectar flow is on; colony does not need the feed
items.fix: Remove the feeder and check again in a week. Bees rarely take syrup when good forage is available.

items.symptom: Bees fighting at the hive entrance, robber bees in flight
items.cause: Syrup smell is attracting robbers from other colonies or wasps
items.fix: Reduce the entrance to one bee-width. Check the feeder is sealed with no leaks around the edges. Feed in the evening when foraging has stopped.

items.symptom: Syrup has gone cloudy or smells sour
items.cause: Fermentation, usually from thin 1:1 syrup sitting too long in warm weather
items.fix: Discard and make a fresh batch. Do not feed fermented syrup. Add a small pinch of citric acid per litre to fresh batches if you are making syrup in hot weather, as it slows fermentation without harming the bees.
